text: Speech BCIs represent a new approach to enable persons with paralysis to communicate through conversing by translating their speech-related neural activity into words or synthetic speech. Previous speech BCI studies that relied on brain-computed auditory speech features or on electrocerebral activity have generally failed to attain reliable and high accuracy speech recognition for open vocabulary and unconstrained sentences. Here, we introduce a high performance speech to text BCI that uses intracortical recordings with a microelectrode array to capture activity of individual neurons corresponding to speech-related articulator movements, and is capable of achieving fast and accurate transcription of speech. Our paralysed participant, an ALS patient who lost the ability to speak purposefully and meaningfully, achieved a 9.1% word error rate on the 50-word “WER50” test—three times better than the previous best speech BCI performance on open vocabulary speech recognition. He also achieved a 24% word error rate on the WER125k—what we believe to be a first in large vocabulary BCI-based speech recognition performance. His BCI could achieve a speech recognition rate of 62 words per minute, 3.4 times faster than the previous BCI performance, which is within an order of magnitude of the fast speech rate in humans. We also identified two salient features of speech neural code underlying the high BCI performance in terms of the spatial distribution of speech-related neural activity as well as the long term persistence of speech phone features post-paralysis. Our work provides a feasible solution for persons with paralysis to restore the ability to communicate efficiently and effectively through speech.
